title: Building Basics
description: Master the art of construction in Minecraft! From cozy houses to towering skyscrapers, bridges, and beautiful gardens.
author: CraftMind

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Your First House
lessons.description: Everyone needs shelter! Let's build a cozy starter house with walls, a roof, windows, and a door.
objectives: Build 4 walls with an opening for a door

steps.description: Walk to the building site at the village square
Look at the blueprint signs — they show a top-down view of the house layout
Lay a 7×5 foundation of oak planks (7 wide, 5 deep)
Build 4-block-high walls on the foundation using cobblestone — leave a 2-block gap in the front for the door
Add oak log pillars at each corner for structural detail
Build a roof using stairs — place them on the top of the walls sloping upward to a ridge line
Place glass panes in 2 of the walls for windows, and an oak door in the front gap
Decorate the inside! Add a crafting table, furnace, and a bed. Make it feel like home! 🏠

quiz.question: What block is commonly used for house foundations in Minecraft?
quiz.explanation: Oak planks (or any wooden planks) are a classic foundation material — they look clean, are easy to get, and match most building styles.

quiz.question: How do you make glass panes?
quiz.explanation: Smelt sand in a furnace to get glass, then place 6 glass blocks in the crafting table (2 rows of 3) to make 16 glass panes.

quiz.question: What's the minimum interior height for a comfortable house?
quiz.explanation: 3 blocks high gives you room to walk (2 blocks) plus headroom. Any less and you can't jump or place blocks overhead. 4 blocks feels spacious!

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Tower Building
lessons.description: Reach for the sky! Build a sturdy watchtower with multiple floors and a lookout platform.
objectives: Create a lookout platform at the top
Use different materials for visual interest

steps.description: Head to the tower construction yard
Study the tower model — notice the stone brick base, alternating stone and wood, and the overhanging top
Build a 5×5 base, 4 blocks high, using stone bricks
Continue upward with oak logs at corners and cobblestone walls for 4 more blocks (total 8 high)
Add ladders on one interior wall for access to all floors
Create a lookout platform at the top: extend the walls 1 block outward, add fence railings, and place torches
Add a banner or flag at the very top of your tower! Make it uniquely yours! 🏰

quiz.question: Why do builders alternate between different block types in towers?
quiz.explanation: Mixing materials like stone and wood creates visual bands and contrast that make buildings look more interesting and realistic.

quiz.question: What's the purpose of fence railings on a tower top?
quiz.explanation: Fence railings prevent you (and other entities) from falling off the edge. In survival, a fall from a tall tower can be fatal!

lessons.title: Lesson 3: Bridge Construction
lessons.description: Connect two points with a beautiful bridge! Learn about spans, support pillars, and arches.
objectives: Plan a bridge path between two points

steps.description: Go to the canyon crossing site
Look at the canyon — notice the two cliff edges. Your bridge will span this gap!
Build stone pillar supports every 5 blocks along the bridge path, extending down to the water level
Lay the bridge deck using spruce planks — make it 3 blocks wide for comfortable walking
Add oak fence railings on both sides of the bridge
Place lanterns or torches on the support pillars for night-time visibility
Add decorative arches under the bridge using stone stairs! Make it look like a Roman aqueduct! 🌉

quiz.question: How far apart should support pillars be on a bridge?
quiz.explanation: Supports every 3-5 blocks keep the bridge looking sturdy and realistic. Too few and it looks flimsy; too many and it's cluttered.

quiz.question: What block type is ideal for underwater bridge supports?
quiz.explanation: Stone, cobblestone, or prismarine are great for underwater supports — they don't wash away like sand or gravel, and prismarine fits the water theme perfectly.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Garden Design
lessons.description: Create a beautiful garden with paths, flowers, trees, and a water feature. Landscaping mastery awaits!
objectives: Plan a garden layout with paths
Add a small pond or fountain

steps.description: Walk to the garden plot behind the village
Look at the garden plan laid out with colored wool — each color represents a different zone
Create winding paths using gravel or coarse dirt — make them curve naturally, not straight lines!
Plant flower beds along the paths: mix different flowers (roses, daisies, tulips) for variety
Add oak and birch trees at planned positions — vary the heights for a natural look
Dig a small pond (4×4, 1 deep), line with prismarine, and fill with water using a water bucket
Place sea lanterns or glowstone around the garden and under the water for magical night lighting
Add a bench (slabs + stairs), a small gazebo, or anything else that makes this garden special! 🌸

quiz.question: Why do garden paths usually curve instead of going straight?
options: Curves look more natural and inviting
Straight paths don't work in Minecraft
quiz.answer: Curves look more natural and inviting
quiz.explanation: In real landscaping and in Minecraft, curved paths look organic and invite exploration. Straight lines feel rigid and artificial — save those for roads!

quiz.question: What's a good way to make trees look natural in a garden?
options: Plant them in a perfect grid
quiz.explanation: Nature is messy! Varying tree heights, spacing them unevenly, and mixing tree types (oak + birch + spruce) creates a much more realistic and appealing garden.

quiz.question: What block emits light underwater?
quiz.explanation: Sea lanterns and glowstone both emit light underwater, creating a beautiful glowing pond effect. Sea lanterns have a softer, more elegant glow.
